title: The Return of the Department Store Edit
verdict: Department stores that curate like independent boutiques will command premium rents again.
evidence: Several legacy department store operators have begun restructuring their floor plans around branded concession models, ceding square footage control to emerging designers and niche labels in exchange for revenue-sharing arrangements. Early results from Nordstrom's concept floors and Selfridges' rotating residency program suggest that dwell time has increased by as much as 40% in curated zones versus traditional merchandise departments. The shift marks a structural move away from wholesale buying toward editorial selection, with store teams increasingly resembling gallery directors rather than category managers.
frame: For landlords, the implication is straightforward: the department store anchor is no longer a single credit tenant but a platform that generates foot traffic through cultural programming. Buildings that accommodate this model, with flexible infrastructure, higher ceilings, and frequent facade refreshes, will attract the operators willing to pay for the privilege. The old calculus of anchor rent per square foot gives way to a more interesting question: what is the co-tenancy premium when the anchor itself becomes a destination?
